results.summary: The feed now moves to the next argument on its own when a card finishes playing.
results.detailed_description:
You were right that the feed stopped dead at the end of every clip. Finishing a card only *unlocked* the next one — it never moved you there, so you had to pick the phone up and swipe to hear the next argument. The one exception was the very last clip of a question, which already rolled onto the next question's card automatically.

I turned that lone exception into the general rule. Any card that plays all the way through now scrolls to the next one on its own: the question card hands you the first argument, each argument hands you the next, and the last argument of a question still hands you the next question's card. So the feed plays hands-free from end to end.

Two things I deliberately kept. The listen-before-you-land gate is untouched — the auto-advance unlocks the next page before moving to it, and double-checks it's unlocked, so it can't be used to skip ahead. And only the card you're actually ON advances you: if a clip finishes behind you (you scrolled back, or you swiped away while it was still playing), the feed stays where you put it rather than jumping.

I typechecked the whole Discover/Services/Models source set against the iOS SDK, but I didn't build or run the app — that's vigil's job. What to watch for on the phone: that the hand-off between clips feels right, and specifically whether it's too abrupt. If it wants a beat of silence between arguments, that's a one-line change and I'd rather you tell me how long it should be than guess.
results.identified_issue: DiscoverView already had this behaviour, but only for one page: advanceIfQuestionFinished auto-scrolled to the next question's card after the LAST clip of a question. Every other card only called unlockNextPage, which unlocks the next page without moving to it — so the listener had to swipe. I generalized that one special case into advanceToNextPage(afterHearing:), which fires for any page (question card or clip) that plays to its natural end. Order matters: unlockNextPage runs first, because handleLanding bounces you off any page past maxAllowedIndex, so advancing before unlocking would land and immediately bounce back. advanceToNextPage still re-checks 'idx + 1 <= maxAllowedIndex' itself rather than trusting the unlock, so the listen-before-you-land gate stays enforced in one place even if the two ever get out of step. This relies on @State read-after-write inside the same closure (maxAllowedIndex reads the unlockedIndex we just wrote) — that pattern is already load-bearing in restore() in this same file, so it isn't new. The 'currentPageID == pageID' guard is kept from the old function: it stops a clip that finishes BEHIND the listener (one they scrolled back from, or one still playing when they swiped away) from yanking the feed. Alternatives rejected: (1) a delay/pause between cards — the request said go to the next argument, and the snappy animation already reads as a beat; (2) auto-advancing on hasFullyHeardCurrent instead of finishedItemID — finishedItemID is the id-carrying signal and can't credit the wrong card. Not verified on device: I typechecked the whole Discover/Services/Models source set against the iOS SDK, but I did not build or run the app (the flow forbids building; vigil deploys). The scroll animation itself and how it feels between clips is the thing to watch on the phone.

description: When a bit of audio finishes playing, we should then go to the next argument.
